Meanwhile, at least one faculty member was excited about the protest, writing in a text, "This Walk out thing is going to be FUN!"

The morning of the protest, Sinagua Middle School Principal Christopher Koenker sent an email that FUSD "supports our students’ constitutional rights to peaceful assembly and free expression and is aware of student-led events planned."

He wrote that "schools will neither encourage nor discourage student participation." He added that the district was looking into providing additional staff and law enforcement presence "for protection and supervision, not to suppress student expression."

After the protest, an internal "Walk Out Debrief" memo emphasized that "school isn’t responsible for students’ safety after they leave campus."

Another memo noted that one teacher, identified as "Ms. Donovan," took her class outside to witness "rights vs. responsibilities" on display at the walkout. An administrator noted that just three students remained inside and that "later conversations with the 3 students indicated that they really weren’t comfortable being outside." The administrator noted she eventually asked Donovan to bring the class back inside "as it might appear that she was influencing them to be outside."

LAURA INGRAHAM: TEACHING US HISTORY HAS BECOME AN EXERCISE IN 'SELF-LOATHING AND RESENTMENT'

In the same memo, the administrator noted that Donovan also discussed ICE operations in her class and had told students that officers had "used" a five-year-old "to lure the family out to take him." The administrator wrote that they had asked a student if she felt Donovan "pushes her opinions on her." The student answered that after the protest Donovan had asked students to raise their hands if they participated in the protest after which she "congratulated them," saying, "Great job for standing up for your first amendment rights."

Parents, however, quickly expressed that they felt outraged and blindsided.

One mother wrote that after learning of the protest, she and her husband "were terrified that our daughter was peer pressured (by friends, classmates, teachers, and the school district) to be a part of this."

"We’re thankful that our daughter remained at school, due to fear and frustration, but there is still the ‘what if’? What if something happened to any students during the walkout?" she continued, adding, "We expect the district to be transparent and not jeopardize our children’s safety."

Another middle school parent wrote that they were not made aware of the protest or contacted until the end of the day. They wrote that their child "was under the impression this was a school sponsored event," given that faculty allegedly provided "maps" and directions to the event.

"This school district has broken my trust and neglected your responsibilities and disregard for your own policies," the parent wrote.

Commenting on this, America First Legal Senior Counsel James Rogers told Fox News Digital that "What these documents show is a stunning betrayal of that trust."

Rogers said that FUSD’s "conduct raises serious questions about whether district employees crossed that line."

"Parents deserved to know where their children were. The law required these officials to act, and they failed on both counts," he said.

item.title: Ex-counterterrorism official Joe Kent continues calling for US military withdrawal from Middle East
item.description: Former National Counterterrorism Center Director Joe Kent is continuing to advocate for the withdrawal of U.S. forces from the Middle East.
item.content:encoded:
Former National Counterterrorism Center Director Joe Kent is reiterating his call for the withdrawal of US forces from the Middle East amid the ongoing war with Iran.

"Get our troops out now before we suffer a mass casualty attack. Break the escalation trap. Trump still has the political strength at home to declare victory & have it resonate, that ability will be lost if we suffer more casualties. Now is the time to leave, immediately," he wrote Sunday on X.

Fox News Digital reached out to the White House on Monday.

US SERVICE MEMBER KILLED IN IRAQ AS TROOPS RECOVER UNIDENTIFIED REMAINS IN JORDAN: CENTCOM

Kent made the comments while sharing a post from U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM).

"Yesterday, U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) announced the passing of two U.S. service members and the missing status of one in Jordan following an Iranian attack on July 17. After a thorough search, U.S. military personnel found unidentified remains at the location earlier today. An examination process to verify the remains is ongoing," CENTCOM noted in part of a statement on Sunday.

"Separately, a U.S. service member in northern Iraq was killed in action July 18 during a controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian one-way attack drone. A second service member was wounded and continues to receive medical treatment for a minor injury," the statement noted.

EX-COUNTERTERRORISM OFFICIAL JOE KENT ENDORSES GOP SENATE PRIMARY CHALLENGER AS TRUMP BACKS LINDSEY GRAHAM

Kent quit the counterterrorism government post earlier this year, citing his opposition to the Iran war.

In part of his resignation letter he asserted, "Iran posed no imminent threat to our nation, and it is clear that we started this war due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby."

EX-COUNTERTERRORISM CHIEF WARNS OF 'MAJOR PROBLEM' THAT COULD FORCE US 'BACK INTO THE WAR ON IRAN'S TERMS'

In a portion of a Saturday post on X he wrote, "We restore order by ending the conflict on our terms: deprive Iran of military targets to strike by removing our troops, bases, & naval forces from the area. Our bases in the region are liabilities & relics of the past—abandoning them is not retreat, it’s strategic adaptation to modern warfare. This move removes Iran’s primary justification for disrupting shipping in the SOH and for attacking its neighbors. We can then offer the carrot: sanctions relief in exchange for freedom of navigation in the SOH."

item.title: Katie Uhlaender: Athletes risk paying the price if Protect College Sports Act is passed
item.description: A five-time Olympian argues the Protect College Sports Act would grant the NCAA an antitrust exemption while stripping athlete rights under Title IX.
item.content:encoded:
The United States Olympic and Paralympic Committee sent out an email asking athletes to support the Protect College Sports Act (PCSA). It is being marketed as a way to protect college sports, but the real question should be: who is protecting the athletes?

Without athletes, there is no sport.

Giving absolute power to a centralized institution feels more like an economic cartel than fair competition. And it will be the athletes who pay the price.

I PLAYED IN THE NFL. WE DON’T WANT WASHINGTON TRYING TO FIX COLLEGE SPORTS

Fair competition is what makes sport great. The PCSA gives the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) complete control over who gets to compete. Is that fair to the athletes and fans who pay to watch and participate?

As a five-time Olympian, I have spent more than two decades competing in a system that promised fair competition and athlete protection. Like every athlete, I trusted those institutions to uphold the integrity of sport. Too often, they haven’t.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

In 2014, Russia was proven to have cheated in the Sochi Olympic Games. I was awarded an Olympic medal on paper, only to have mine – and 27 others – returned to Russia through a flawed arbitration process. Leading into the 2026 Olympics, an independent tribunal found that Canada intentionally manipulated Olympic qualification points, yet nothing was done. Most have heard of the Larry Nassar scandal, where the USOPC and NCAA failed to protect athletes despite years of complaints. Not to mention the recent controversy surrounding Imane Khelif, an Olympic boxer classified as having Difference of Sex Development (DSD). Khelif reportedly has XY chromosomes, but won women's gold at the Paris Olympics, raising questions about competitive integrity and institutional accountability.

These are different scandals with different players, but they reveal the same pattern: justice delayed and justice denied. Congress is considering granting the NCAA a federal antitrust exemption, in exchange for the NCAA promising to regulate itself. But what has the NCAA done to demonstrate that it is capable of policing itself? Today, the NCAA and its member universities have more than 260 open Title IX investigations involving the mishandling of sexual assault and misconduct. Congress and the Supreme Court have repeatedly been forced to intervene because the NCAA has failed to self-regulate. Why should we believe that giving the NCAA more power and legal protection would suddenly produce a different result? More importantly, how does this protect athletes?

The PCSA would ultimately strip athletes of the ability to challenge the NCAA and allow the organization to define what constitutes "reasonable" action to prevent abuse. Institutions that have failed to protect athletes should not receive additional legal protections and more power. Giving more power without meaningful accountability creates a dangerous imbalance. History shows that power imbalances lead to abuse, which is why antitrust laws exist.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Without the private right to challenge these institutions and their policies, athletes are left vulnerable to scandal. The more than 260 open Title IX cases involving sexual assault and misconduct would be neutralized with this act passing, leaving institutions without accountability, and athletes harmed. The act grants collective media rights to schools that join, allowing those currently facing Title IX non-compliance claims to remain non-compliant with no oversight of compliance, and the athletes who challenged them without power.

This shifts the focus from proactively preventing abuse to bureaucratic box-checking designed to block lawsuits; not protecting athletes.

The consequences extend beyond abuse prevention. The act grants the NCAA absolute control over athlete eligibility through this antitrust shield, undermining federal and state law, specifically Title IX and who can compete in the women’s category. The NCAA can now determine eligibility rules with no accountability, no oversight on compliance, and no input from the states or athletes. This means current abuse goes unpunished, and that the NCAA can also allow men to compete in women’s sports again.

The current NCAA policies define gender as an identity, do not define sex, and only require a birth certificate as proof of being male or female. Forty-four states currently allow birth certificates to be amended, and there is no oversight or verification of these documents. Male athletes who identify as female still earn scholarships, use women’s locker rooms, and play and compete on women’s teams. The 27 states that have chosen to recognize biological women as a protected class could do nothing to stop men from taking women’s spots on college sports teams.

The act may create stability for the NCAA and protect it from lawsuits, but stability without accountability has historically led to scandal and abuse of power. The Olympic system has faced this pattern repeatedly.

Women have spent more than 50 years fighting for equal opportunity and legal protections. Title IX is a symbol of that progress. Congress should not weaken those protections by passing this bill as it is currently written.

Our Constitution was built on the expectation that citizens would exercise their freedom of speech, stand up for what they believe in, and help shape public policy. Any public policy that removes a citizen’s ability to advocate for themselves is un-American. And in this case, the proposed policy is simply a proposal to hand financial wins to the elite governing class while guaranteeing generations of sexual, physical, and economic abuse of the athletes.

I've lived through the system of internal arbitration, athlete board representation, and ombudsman programs, and they often create conflicts of interest while essentially just protecting the organization from meaningful accountability. Because these are internal systems that exist with an inherent imbalance of power, they almost always digress into tools to prevent disruption of the economic system. Athletes are told that policies will be changed for future generations, giving their own suffering some meaning. But these promises don’t restore lost opportunities, protect those already harmed, or deliver justice. They delay accountability while athletes continue to bear the consequences. Justice delayed is justice denied.

Stability without accountability is not protection.

It’s exploitation.

item.title: Hawley sends criminal referral of 'left-wing dark-money' group over mailed abortion drugs
item.description: Sen. Josh Hawley alleges Plan C operates as a referral hub shipping abortion pills into states where they're banned, urging a criminal probe.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: A Republican senator says that a left-wing abortion group claiming to be a "public health information campaign" is actually operating as the referral hub for shipping abortion pills into states where they're banned, in a criminal referral obtained by Fox News Digital.

In a letter sent Thursday to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, Sen. Josh Hawley alleges asked the Justice Department to open a criminal investigation into Plan C, saying the group connects women with abortion-pill manufacturers, foreign clinics and online vendors that ship the drugs into states where they are restricted by law.

"At the center of the effort is an entity called Plan C," Hawley wrote, describing the group as a "left-wing dark-money operation" that serves as the "referral and logistics hub" for distributing abortion drugs into states that prohibit them. He said the group's activities warrant a federal criminal investigation.

BLUE STATE SHIELD LAWS ALLOWED 330K ABORTION PILLS TO BE SENT TO ABORTION BAN STATES, PRO-LIFE GROUP FINDS

Hawley says Plan C directs women in states such as Florida, Oklahoma and Texas to foreign clinics willing to mail abortion pills into the United States despite state laws restricting those shipments. He also says the group points users to online vendors selling abortion drugs without prescriptions, identification or medical consultations, even though Plan C acknowledges on its website that the pills sold through some of those sites are "not regulated or inspected by the US government."

Fox News Digital reached out to Plan C for comment.

Hawley's letter also says Plan C advises users on how to limit their "digital footprint" by recommending privacy-focused browsers, virtual private networks and encrypted email.

The senator notes that Plan C's own website warns users they could face legal consequences for obtaining abortion pills through the services it recommends, while saying it is "up to every individual to decide what level of legal risk they are willing to take."

HAWLEY URGES DOJ PROBE INTO ABORTION PILL MAKER OVER SAFETY CONCERNS

Hawley also says Plan C directs women to providers willing to prescribe abortion pills through 14 weeks of pregnancy — beyond the Food and Drug Administration's approved labeling for mifepristone and beyond the limits set by several states.

Hawley argues the conduct could violate multiple federal laws, including provisions of the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act covering prescription drugs, laws prohibiting the importation of unapproved drugs into interstate commerce, and federal conspiracy and aiding-and-abetting statutes.

"I therefore respectfully refer this matter to the Department to open a criminal investigation into Plan C, its relationship with potential co-conspirator manufacturers, and the network of suppliers it promotes," Hawley wrote, adding that the investigation should include foreign clinics and online vendors shipping abortion drugs into the United States.

item.title: FBI boss Kash Patel details how agency and partners secured record-shattering FIFA World Cup
item.description: In a Fox News Digital exclusive, FBI Director Kash Patel details the massive World Cup security operation, including 700 drone takedowns and no significant attacks.
item.content:encoded:
EXCLUSIVE – Seventy-eight Super Bowls in 38 days. This was the scale of the task facing the Federal Bureau of Investigation going into the first World Cup final the United States has hosted in more than 30 years.

"That’s what we executed on," FBI Director Kash Patel told Fox News Digital in an exclusive interview detailing the largest commitment of the agency’s resources ever devoted to securing a sporting event – the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

"We’re proud to report that, thank God, and thankfully to the men and women of the FBI and our partners, we had no significant criminal attacks during this World Cup," Patel said. "And just to put that in perspective, we had 6.5 million people attend the 78 matches in the U.S. across 11 venues."

Patel said the planning and execution had to be "mathematically precise."

FIFA WORLD CUP COMES TO THRILLING END WITH WINNING GOAL SCORED IN EXTRA TIME

The tournament shattered previous attendance records and brought millions of international visitors to the country. The FBI dedicated nearly 5,000 personnel to the effort, including more than 4,000 from field offices, 800 from FBI headquarters and two legal attaché offices, along with support from 59 Homeland Security task forces. 

The bureau also conducted approximately 300,000 background checks on players, coaches and other accredited personnel. All told, nearly 7 million fans from all over the world attended World Cup games in the United States, Canada and Mexico, all with no reported major security incidents. In fact, getting into the stadiums often took fans less than 15 minutes for games that averaged more than 65,000 in house.

A defining element of the FBI’s security operation was its aggressive response to the rapidly evolving drone threat. Recognizing that even a low-cost unmanned aircraft could be used to surveil players practicing at the dozens of team base camps around the country, track their movements to and from hotels, probe for security tactics at stadiums or fan zones, disrupt an event or deliver an explosive or chemical device, the bureau treated every unauthorized incursion as a potential threat.

TRUMP TAKES IN JENNIFER HUDSON'S INCREDIBLE NATIONAL ANTHEM PERFORMANCE BEFORE WORLD CUP FINAL

"If you’re in the space, we’re gonna take you down. And that’s why we took down over 700," Patel said. "Some of these folks were maybe borderline nuisances, while many of them were criminal in terms of violations of FAA airspace and also failing to register their drones… We’re not taking any chances."

The FBI established its first dedicated counter-UAS (unmanned aircraft system) school at Redstone Arsenal in Alabama to train state and local officers in detecting, tracking and intercepting unauthorized drones. Those officers were then deployed on FBI-led ground-intercept teams at World Cup venues, creating a nationwide counter-drone capability built for a threat that barely existed when the U.S. last hosted the tournament in 1994.

"We, the FBI, specifically train for the worst of the worst," Patel said, pointing to the possibility of drones being used to deploy explosives or chemical agents. Former FBI Deputy Director Dan Bongino was instrumental in developing the bureau’s counter-drone program.

That preparation was tested as the World Cup was getting underway, when investigators uncovered an alleged coordinated attack targeting UFC Freedom 250 at the White House.

5 CHILLING DETAILS FROM THE ALLEGED WHITE HOUSE ATTACK PLOT TIED TO UFC EVENT

Federal prosecutors allege the suspects planned to use explosive-laden drones to trigger panic and force crowds to evacuate into areas where armed attackers could strike. Patel said the FBI launched a six-state operation, tracked the alleged threat through online communications and began a coordinated takedown "before the first punch was thrown," allowing the event to proceed without an attack.

Eight defendants have since been charged, although the allegations remain unproven and the defendants are presumed innocent.

FBI REVEALS WHY TRUMP WHITE HOUSE UFC EVENT WENT AHEAD DESPITE ALLEGED TERROR PLOT

International cooperation was another critical component of the World Cup security operation.

"They’re working overseas in their home locations and giving us information back in real time on any single threat that’s coming in," Patel said.

The FBI hosted the International Police Cooperation Center in Northern Virginia, deploying more than 70 personnel to work alongside officers from U.S. host cities and security officials representing the nations sending teams to the tournament. 

The FBI also assembled a Joint Operations Center with foreign police representatives from 46 of the 48 participating countries, allowing investigators to receive real-time threat intelligence from officials who understood their teams, fan bases and domestic security concerns. That information was supported by the FBI’s Intelligence Coordination Center, where more than 400 personnel processed an average of 26 incidents per day and produced nearly 100 intelligence reports for federal, state, local and private-sector partners, according to the FBI.

Patel credited the White House Task Force on the FIFA World Cup with bringing federal, state and local agencies together under a unified security strategy. Established by President Donald Trump and led by Andrew Giuliani’s team, the task force served as a central hub for interagency planning, intelligence sharing and the rapid deployment of law enforcement resources across the tournament’s 11 U.S. host cities.

FIRST WORLD CUP HALFTIME SHOW DRAWS MIXED REACTIONS FOR SHAKIRA, JUSTIN BIEBER, BTS, MADONNA PERFORMANCES

"Like anything in government, to succeed, you need tremendous leadership," Patel said. "President Trump had the foresight to establish the White House Task Force… and what they were able to do was bring in the interagency, at least at the federal level, and then work with us at the state and local levels through our partnerships and put everybody under one roof."

Patel said the coordinated structure allowed the FBI to quickly send bomb technicians, evidence teams, tactical operators, SWAT personnel and hostage-rescue capabilities wherever additional resources were needed.

FOX WORLD CUP ANNOUNCER SAYS TRUMP'S APPEARANCE AT FINAL IS 'FUN, UNIQUE THING' DESPITE POTENTIAL BACKLASH

"President Trump set the standard: Not only were we going to have the largest and safest sporting event in the history of the world, but we were going to make sure everyone had a great time. Those were the marching orders," Patel said.

He pointed to the social media videos showing international fans enjoying restaurants, pubs, sporting venues and other distinctly American experiences — even joking that Europeans fell in love with ranch dressing.

VIRAL GERMAN SOCCER FAN FREDDY INVITED FOR WHITE HOUSE VISIT AS FAIRY-TALE TRIP ACROSS AMERICA CONTINUES

"These are small vignettes, but they quantify and qualify how awesome America did," Patel said. "And I don’t just mean government. I mean the private sector. They came in and partnered with us as well."

Patel said those partnerships helped generate billions of dollars in revenue across the country while giving visitors a memorable experience.

item.title: Americans divided after Will Arnett blasts one of the country's most popular french fries
item.description: Will Arnett sparked a heated debate over shoestring fries, but a YouGov survey reveals steak fries and curly fries are the top overall picks.
item.content:encoded:
Americans love french fries and rank them among their favorite foods. When it comes to the preferred cut and cooking method, however, there's plenty of debate.

Actor Will Arnett sparked a lively discussion online when he expressed his disdain for shoestring fries.

"Everybody hates shoestring fries," Arnett said on the "Off Menu" podcast.

ALTON BROWN REVEALS THE ONE CHILDHOOD SNACK HE'D INCLUDE IN HIS IDEAL LAST MEAL

Shoestring fries, also known as matchstick fries, are cut extremely thin.

"If you serve me what they call shoestring fries, we have a problem," Arnett said, arguing that they "aren't fries" and claiming that restaurants serving them are being lied to by the people around them.

"So glad someone has finally spoken out about shoestring fries," one commenter on the podcast's YouTube clip wrote.

"Will is right, shoestring fries are [expletive]," another wrote.

Others said they prefer shoestring fries.

"Noooo shoestring fries are the best! People like them I swear! They're so crispy!" one person commented.

"Shoesting fries are phenomenal. [Crinkle]-cut fries should be illegal," someone else wrote.

A 2022 YouGov survey of 1,000 adults found that 22% preferred thicker-cut steak fries the most.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

Curly fries, which are sliced into a spiral shape, were the top pick for 21% of respondents, while 21% said they had no preference for the type of fry.

Crinkle-cut fries, which use a corrugated blade to create grooves in the potato, were favored by 19% of respondents.

Waffle fries, which are cut into a grid pattern with holes in the potato slices, garnered 12% of the vote, while 6% of respondents said "not applicable."

The history of the french fry is somewhat vague. Food vendors were selling fried potato strips in Paris by the late 18th century, though some claim Belgian villagers had invented them before that.

Many historians believe American soldiers encountered french fries in Belgium during World War I, where French was widely spoken, helping popularize the name.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

In YouGov's 2026 rankings of favorite American dishes, fries ranked second, just behind grilled cheese. Eighty-five percent of people reported liking fries, while 11% said they were neutral. Only 4% said they disliked fries.

French fries were the second-most-popular food in YouGov's 2026 rankings of favorite American dishes, just behind grilled cheese. Eighty-five percent of people reported liking fries, while 11% said they were neutral. Only 4% said they disliked fries.

The average American eats nearly 34 pounds of fries each year, according to the U.S. Department of Commerce.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

McDonald's serves America's favorite fast-food fries, according to the 2026 YouGov survey, with 39.2% of respondents choosing the chain. Five Guys (9%) and Chick-fil-A (7.1%) ranked second and third, respectively.

Digital creator Bobby Parrish recently posted on Instagram a comparison of three iconic fries, judging them for taste, ingredients and overall quality.

Parrish ranked McDonald's fries last, arguing that the chain uses several seed oils, including hydrogenated soybean oil, which Parrish said is "one of the worst."

McDonald's fries also contain a natural beef flavor made with wheat and milk derivatives, Parrish noted. Therefore, they are not suitable for vegans and are not considered gluten-free.

Parrish ranked Chick-fil-A's waffle fries, which he said are made with canola and palm oil, as the second-best.

Steak 'n Shake's french fry ingredients are "darn near perfect," Parrish said. The burger-and-fry chain promotes its use of 100% beef tallow at its restaurants.

item.title: RNC trolls DNC, Maine Democratic Party in letter offering to help restore trust, 'defend democracy’
item.description: The RNC sent a tongue-in-cheek letter offering election lawyers and trained observers to Democrats as Maine scrambles to replace Graham Platner.
item.content:encoded:
The Republican National Committee is trolling the Democratic National Committee and the Maine Democratic Party with an offer to provide election lawyers, trained observers and other resources as Democrats scramble to replace disgraced Senate primary candidate Graham Platner.

"So, in a spirit of generosity we hope will not go unnoticed, the RNC formally offers our election integrity resources and personnel to ensure a process worthy of Democrat voters’ confidence," read a copy of the letter obtained exclusively by Fox News Digital. "Mainers deserve a nomination that is fair, accessible, and free from disenfranchisement — and it never hurts to have professionals in the room."

The tongue-in-cheek letter is expected to be sent Monday morning from the RNC to DNC chair Ken Martin and Maine Democratic Party Chair Charles Dingman, the RNC election integrity unit told Fox News Digital.

PLATNER ALLY TROY JACKSON CLOSING IN ON DEMOCRATIC SENATE NOD IN KEY RACE TO FACE COLLINS

The letter unwinds many of the Democratic Party platform's attacks on Republicans in a week that featured an unusual Democratic Party debate and President Donald Trump's primetime White House address on election integrity.

"The RNC has a deep bench of experienced election attorneys, trained observers, a dedicated State Election Integrity Director, and nationwide election integrity resources — all standing by to assist with convention procedures, delegate credentialing, ballot-counting transparency, and election law compliance," the letter read, pointing to its multi-million dollar midterm election integrity ground game. "We built this operation to protect elections from exactly this kind of chaos. We simply never expected the loudest cries for help to come from inside your own house.

"As our friends on the left never tire of reminding us, 'democracy dies in darkness.' We are offering to turn on the lights."

MEET THE DEMOCRATS GUNNING TO REPLACE GRAHAM PLATNER

Republicans have received criticism for questioning election integrity, but the RNC letter attempted to turn the table back on the Democrats, during a key Senate race in Maine that could help determine the Senate majority this November.

"We also request clarification on whether your nominating convention will include the basic safeguards every election deserves: voter identification, proof of residency, and independent observation," the letter continued, pointing to apparent hypocrisy. "We ask in a spirit of thoroughness, not suspicion. Mostly thoroughness."

"We further understand that proxy voting will not be permitted," the letter said. "I’m pleased to hear your party discover, at last, the merits of in-person voting — and to learn that when the stakes are highest for Democrats, you too believe there is no substitute for showing up and casting a ballot yourself."

When Platner suspended his Senate campaign before the July deadline after winning the nomination in June, he called out the party's "establishment" for forcing its will over the will of voters.

"For his part, Mr. Platner announced the suspension of his campaign by insisting that his replacement come from "'the people of Maine' through a system that is 'open, transparent and democratic' and that reflects the voices of those who supported him," the RNC letter noted, adding a dig on the DNC's reported financial struggles, suggesting its political opponents should accept their offer of help.

"We also know that this convention comes at a difficult time financially for you, with the DNC $18 million in debt. Finding the resources necessary to run a nominating convention must be tough."

item.title: ANDREW GIULIANI: How President Trump’s World Cup vision showed America at its very best
item.description: The White House Task Force on the 2026 FIFA World Cup deployed $625 million in security funding and $500 million in counter-drone technology.
item.content:encoded:
When President Donald Trump set his sights on bringing the FIFA World Cup back to the United States in 2018, he wasn’t just thinking about soccer. He was thinking about legacy—about uniting a country, welcoming the world and showing what American excellence looks like as we celebrate our nation’s 250th birthday.

I remember the moment he gave me my main mission: "Make it the best tournament ever." Simple, but not easy. With President Trump, nothing is ever done halfway. Early in his second term, he established the White House Task Force on the 2026 FIFA World Cup, making clear this would be a whole-of-government effort. We set out to deliver not only the largest and safest World Cup, but also the most welcoming and memorable tournament in history for fans, athletes, and communities across America.

Looking back, I know only President Trump could have pulled this off. No other leader could have set such an ambitious vision and inspired a nation to rise to the challenge. His lifelong experience in hospitality and his attention to detail set the standard for excellence and made this World Cup unforgettable.

GERMAN WORLD CUP FAN BREAKS DOWN IN TEARS AFTER BEING BLOWN AWAY BY KINDNESS OF AMERICANS

More than 6.5 million fans attended matches across 16 host cities in the U.S., Mexico and Canada, nearly doubling the previous attendance record. There were 11 host cities in the United States alone. During this World Cup, stadiums were filled to 99.7% capacity, while FIFA Fan Festivals welcomed another 7.7 million visitors. Fans from more than 150 countries experienced American hospitality firsthand, and the tournament generated more than 20 billion video views worldwide.

Yet the numbers tell only part of the story. The real success was measured in packed downtowns, thriving small businesses, friendships formed across cultures and unforgettable moments shared by millions.

President Trump’s focus on safety and security paid off. We deployed federal coordination teams, supported local law enforcement with $625 million in funding and invested another $500 million in counter-drone technology. We worked closely with state and local officials, trained together, and built a real-time information-sharing system that will benefit our country long after the final whistle.

FEDS CHARGE FOUR AS WORLD CUP DRONE CRACKDOWN TOPS 400 SEIZURES ACROSS US HOST CITIES NATIONWIDE

The results speak for themselves: no major security incidents, a record-breaking economic impact, and a seamless experience for fans and athletes. From Seattle’s Pioneer Square, where local businesses saw sales rise to as much as 10 times their average, to Philadelphia’s $770 million in projected economic impact, the benefits will last for years to come.

The true legacy is the capability we built. America now has stronger technology, better training, and deeper partnerships at home and abroad. We worked with Canada, Mexico and FIFA to deliver a continental spectacle—proving that no country is better equipped to host an event on this scale. President Trump’s Golden Age of Sports is here, and the lessons learned will inform not only future World Cups, but also every major event we host.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

It’s especially meaningful that President Trump, who was the driving force behind our successful bid in 2018, was able to see this vision realized during his presidency. Few leaders get to witness their ambitions come full circle on a stage this grand. The pride he felt—and that we all shared—as we watched the world come together on American soil is something I’ll never forget.

Serving as executive director of the White House Task Force on the 2026 FIFA World Cup has been the honor of a lifetime. I will always be grateful for the opportunity to serve under President Trump and help make his vision for this country a reality during this extraordinary moment for America.

As we look ahead to the Olympics and America’s next chapter, let this World Cup stand as proof that when President Trump sets an ambitious vision, America delivers. We welcomed the world with unmatched hospitality, world-class venues, unwavering security and the confidence that has always defined this nation at its best. 

Americans fell in love with the World Cup, and the world fell in love with America again. That is the enduring legacy of the 2026 FIFA World Cup and the future of sports in America’s Golden Age.

item.title: LEE CARTER: Why I believe collapsing trust, not ideology, is reshaping American politics
item.description: Zohran Mamdani, Graham Platner and Troy Jackson all represent a deeper crisis of institutional trust, not a simple leftward shift in politics.
item.content:encoded:
After every recent election in which a democratic socialist or self-described progressive outsider scores an upset victory, the same debate erupts: Is America really moving that far to the left?

Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s rise in New York reignited the question. Before his campaign collapsed, Graham Platner’s meteoric rise in Maine sparked similar headlines. And now, as Democrats look to replace him, many are rallying around Troy Jackson, a self-described "pickup truck progressive."

The conventional wisdom is that these victories signal a country embracing increasingly liberal ideas.

DSA ROLLS OUT NEW PLATFORM AHEAD OF MIDTERMS

I don’t think that’s what’s happening.

The rise of democratic socialism isn’t the story.

It’s the symptom.

SOCIALISM’S RISE INSIDE THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY NOW THREATENS THE AMERICAN DREAM

The real story is that millions of Americans have lost confidence in the institutions they once trusted to solve problems, create opportunity and make government work. And when confidence in institutions collapses, voters don’t necessarily move left or right. They become far more willing to embrace candidates who promise to disrupt the status quo.

Gallup’s latest polling should be a flashing warning light. Confidence in America’s institutions remains near historic lows. Congress commands the confidence of just 9% of Americans. Confidence in the criminal justice system, organized religion, higher education, big business and many other institutions Americans once relied upon has eroded dramatically over the past generation.

Look beyond the individual numbers and a striking pattern emerges.

BERNIE-BACKED SOCIALIST WHO ALLIED WITH PLATNER COULD SUPPLANT HIM ON BALLOT

This isn’t a crisis in one institution.

It’s a crisis of confidence in the entire system.

That’s an important distinction because politics doesn’t happen in a vacuum. Voters don’t evaluate candidates based solely on policy positions. They also evaluate whether the institutions surrounding those policies are capable of delivering results.

When Americans stop believing those institutions work, they stop rewarding candidates who promise incremental change.

They start looking for alternatives.

Some find them on the right.

Some find them on the left.

But almost all of them are looking for outsiders.

That’s what made Platner’s campaign so compelling before it unraveled. His appeal wasn’t simply about progressive policy. He represented a rejection of politics as usual. His outsider status became part of his message.

The same is true of Mamdani.

Whether you agree with his policies or not, his supporters aren’t simply voting for a platform. They’re voting for disruption. They’re voting against institutions they believe have failed them.

And look at what happened in Maine after Platner exited the race.

Many Democrats didn’t immediately search for the safest establishment replacement. Instead, attention quickly shifted to Jackson — a candidate who proudly brands himself a "pickup truck progressive" and who has built his career positioning himself as a champion of working people against entrenched interests.

Different state.

Different ideology.

The same political demand.

We’re seeing versions of this story play out across the country.

Candidates who promise to challenge institutions are outperforming candidates who promise simply to manage them.

The labels vary.

The frustration does not.

This is why I believe we continue to misread this political moment.

The defining divide in American politics is no longer simply Democrat versus Republican.

Nor is it liberal versus conservative.

Increasingly, it is establishment versus anti-establishment.

Institution versus outsider.

The rise of democratic socialism is one manifestation of that shift.

Right-wing populism is another.

They are different political expressions of the same underlying condition: millions of Americans no longer believe the institutions around them are capable of solving the problems they face.

That should concern all of us.

A healthy democracy depends on citizens believing that its institutions — while imperfect — are fundamentally legitimate and capable of correcting mistakes, enforcing rules fairly and delivering results.

When that confidence disappears, disruption becomes more attractive than stability.

History tells us that people rarely embrace political movements on the fringe simply because they wake up one day with a new ideology.

They do it because they lose confidence in the institutions that once made moderation feel sufficient.

That is why I don’t believe the rise of democratic socialism is the biggest story in American politics.

The bigger story is the collapse of confidence that made its rise possible.

Until our institutions begin earning back the confidence of the American people, we shouldn’t be surprised to see more candidates like Zohran Mamdani.

Or Graham Platner.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

Or Troy Jackson.

Because they aren’t the cause of this political moment.

They’re the consequence of it.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM LEE HARTLEY CARTER

item.title: JONATHAN TURLEY: Democratic Socialists want to remake America in their image. That’s terrifying
item.description: The Democratic Socialists of America platform calls for eliminating the Senate, Supreme Court and presidency in a plan echoing French Revolution Jacobins.
item.content:encoded:
"Imagine the end of capitalism." Those words in the Democratic Socialists of America manifesto offer Americans a world without landlords, debt, mandatory work hours, and "food, education, energy, medicine, and transportation... are common goods and utilities."

The left often refers to radical changes as "reimagining" everything from police to the Supreme Court to prisons to borders. However, the rise of socialists in the Democratic Party has moved such claims from the merely rhetorical to the outright revolutionary.

The release of the DSA platform on Bastille Day, July 14, was fitting since the Jacobins pushed for the same unicameral system that the socialists are now advocating.The platform promised to change the constitutional system and end the rule of "the same criminal class of billionaires and war profiteers."

BERNIE SANDERS, DSA REVEAL DEMANDS FOR DEM PARTY AFTER SOCIALISTS SWEEP NEW YORK ELECTIONS

The DSA, which counts various Democrat figures such as New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani, includes a plan to eliminate the Senate, the Supreme Court, the presidency and other institutions in remaking the American constitutional system.

In my new book "Rage and the Republic: The Unfinished Story of the American Revolution," I discuss the rise of socialism and the move among professors and pundits to scrap key aspects, or the entirety, of our constitutional system on the 250th anniversary of the republic.

The book compares many of these proposals to identical policies of the Jacobin movement in the French Revolution. It is a book about revolutions and how they can consume those who start them. Both the American and French revolutions arose during the same period, but one became the world’s oldest democracy while the other became a blood-soaked tyranny known as "The Reign of Terror."

THE FOUNDING FATHERS WOULD SOUND THE ALARM ON AOC, MAMDANI AND OUR SLIDE INTO SOCIALISM

Notably, the Jacobins pushed for the same unicameral system that the socialists are now advocating. Even figures such as Thomas Paine (one of two figures who played a significant role in both revolutions) supported such measures to allow for more direct democracy.

It was an approach that the Framers expressly rejected. Great students of history, they did not want to replicate the elements that led to the destruction of past democracies going back to ancient Athens. Benjamin Rush, a signatory to the Declaration of Independence, referred to it as democracies becoming "mobocracies."

The DSA is the direct descendant of the Jacobins in seeking to tear down moderating institutions and replace the president and justices with democratically elected figures by a Congress composed only of the House of Representatives. However, many of these suggestions are also found among leading academics and influencers.

BROADCAST NETWORKS TOUT MAMDANI’S VICTORIES, PROCLAIM SOCIALISM IS ‘RESONATING’

As with the original Jacobins, those demanding these changes are not the working class but professors, pundits and young college-educated voters.

Mainstream figures such as Erwin Chemerinsky, dean of UC Berkeley Law School, have published books such as "No Democracy Lasts Forever: How the Constitution Threatens the United States."

The media eagerly publishes these radical views, such as the New York Times column, "The Constitution Is Broken and Should Not Be Reclaimed," in which Ryan D. Doerfler of Harvard and Samuel Moyn of Yale call for the nation to "reclaim America from constitutionalism."

As discussed in "Rage and the Republic":

"Those who want to remain in favor often pander to the extremes. Radical leftist sites like the Jacobin have grown in popularity... They use some of the same words and symbols but still seem improvisational and inauthentic, like a child’s game of dress-up with oversized outfits. Indeed, that is precisely the danger.

"There is little knowledge, let alone memory, of what such radicalism became under the real Jacobins. There is the same blissful ignorance that allowed Rousseauian dilettantes to mutate into monsters, escalating radical agendas with every failure... As the popular slogans became disastrous policies, the momentum would carry them beyond any point of recognition or return. The new Jacobins show the same blind ambition and abandon in striking out at institutions and the Constitution itself."

Those slogans are now preying on the gullible and the vulnerable. Take the recent pitch of Michaela Brangan, DSA national political education chair, who declared that with socialism:

"[Y]ou are able to take a day off and not have to worry and you don't have to have a job just to pay the bills because you do not have a lot of bills. Right? Bills are no longer a thing because there's not for-profit businesses that are constantly trying to exploit you and trying, you know, to put you into debt."

She laughed at the suggestion of, "Why do we have a Senate? What is so great about the Senate?"

If she wants that answer, she only needs to look around her at the mob seeking to destroy the world's oldest and most successful republic in history.

She is right about one thing. Capitalism has played a critical role in our republic. This is the 250th anniversary of not just our Declaration but the publication of Adam Smith's "Wealth of Nations," which the Founders eagerly embraced. They believed that you could not be truly independent and free if you are not economically independent and free. They rejected the economic dependency pushed by many on the left.

The left has found a voice in the class warfare espoused by Vermont Independent Sen. Bernie Sanders and others. However, it is not a new voice. It is the same Siren's call that we rejected 250 years ago. Paine himself would barely survive in France and come to appreciate the structural precautions that Founding Father and President James Madison created in our system that were absent in the French system.

After the Jacobins implemented some of these very same policies, they destroyed their economy and then devoured themselves in waves of executions. Once again, these same voices are asking a people to destroy the most successful and stable democracy in history. Mainstream figures are conditioning the public for radical changes, often using the same coded messaging. Former Vice President Kamala Harris dismissed the opposition to ideas like packing the Supreme Court as mere "nostalgia" for a system that is no longer working.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

California Gov. Gavin Newsom recently also pandered to the mob in declaring that "nostalgia is not working," and added, "capitalism as we know it doesn't work either."

It is not "nostalgia" that binds us to this constitutional system. It is the faith in a system based on natural rights; a system that protects the minority from the majority. It is a system based on faith in both political and economic independence; the embrace of freedom and free-market principles that created the greatest prosperity in history.

We have heard these voices before and we would be wise to take them seriously if we are, as Benjamin Franklin asked, to keep this Republic for the next generation.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM JONATHAN TURLEY

item.title: AMB. GORDON SONDLAND: Europe can’t condemn Iran, then complain when Trump finally acts to stop it
item.description: Iran's nuclear program, proxy warfare through Hezbollah and Houthis, and ballistic missiles all threaten Europe, yet leaders avoid hard decisions.
item.content:encoded:
European leaders have spent years warning that Iran must never be permitted to acquire a nuclear weapon. They have condemned Tehran’s ballistic-missile program, its financing of terrorism, its use of proxy forces, its attacks on commercial shipping and its serial violations of international agreements.

TRUMP'S 60-DAY IRAN DEAL REACHES HALFWAY MARK AS CEASEFIRE COLLAPSES INTO ESCALATING WAR

Yet when President Donald Trump concluded that the threat required military action, much of Europe’s response focused not on Iran’s conduct, but on whether European capitals had been sufficiently consulted.

That complaint is difficult to take seriously.

European governments were not ignorant of the intelligence picture. Their intelligence services have followed Iran’s nuclear program, missile development, terrorist networks and regional proxies for decades. They have reviewed the same broad threat assessments, watched the same enrichment trends and issued many of the same warnings.

MACRON UNDER FIRE OVER IRAN, HEZBOLLAH POLICY AS TRUMP ADMIN HOSTS ISRAEL-LEBANON TALKS

They may not have possessed every piece of operational intelligence available to the president, but they understood the direction of travel. They knew the danger was growing. They also knew that, eventually, someone would have to act.

Europe’s preferred strategy was to play ostrich: acknowledge the threat, avoid the hard decision, wait for the U.S. to take the lead and then feign surprise at the result.

That arrangement is politically convenient. It allows European leaders to benefit from American power while distancing themselves from its use. Washington absorbs the military risk and political criticism. Europe preserves its diplomatic posture and complains about process.

AMERICANS ARE NOT GIVING UP ON NATO, BUT THEY EXPECT EUROPE TO DO ITS SHARE

But Europe cannot have it both ways.

If Iran is a common threat to the democratic world, as European leaders have repeatedly said, then confronting it cannot remain principally an American responsibility. Europe cannot rely on U.S. military power, criticize Washington for acting, and then expect to enjoy the security benefits of the action without accepting any of the burdens.

This is not merely President Trump’s war, nor is it solely America’s problem. It is a conflict involving the security of the Western world.

Iran’s leaders do not regard the dispute as one between Tehran and Washington alone. They view the U.S., Europe and Israel as parts of the same hostile political and cultural bloc. Iranian intelligence operations have reached European soil. Iranian-backed groups have threatened European citizens and interests. Iranian missiles increasingly place European territory within range. European economies depend on maritime trade routes that Tehran has repeatedly threatened to disrupt.

Europe cannot plausibly believe that by doing little more than it is doing today, Iran will somehow spare it.

Passivity is not neutrality, and it does not purchase immunity. Tehran is unlikely to distinguish between an American adversary and a European government that quietly benefits from American protection. A regime prepared to use terrorism, hostage-taking, maritime coercion and proxy warfare will not reward European hesitation with safety.

The most important contribution Europe can make now does not require firing a shot or spending another euro.

It requires political unity.

There should be no public daylight between the U.S. and its principal European allies over the strategic objectives. Tactical disagreements are inevitable and can be addressed privately. But public equivocation, procedural complaints and ritual calls for "restraint by all sides" are interpreted in Tehran as evidence that the Western alliance can be divided.

That perception encourages Iran to wait out the pressure, manipulate European governments and bet that Atlantic unity will eventually collapse.

The greatest strategic gift the West could give Tehran is the appearance of division. The greatest deterrent it can present is the opposite.

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, French President Emmanuel Macron, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer should state clearly that the U.S. and Europe share the same objectives and will remain aligned until they are achieved.

Those objectives must extend beyond the nuclear issue.

Preventing Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon is essential. But ending Iran’s reliance on proxy armies is equally important.

For decades, Tehran has used Hezbollah, Hamas, the Houthis, Iraqi militias and other armed groups to attack adversaries while maintaining a thin layer of deniability. Proxy warfare has allowed Iran to impose enormous costs on the region without accepting the consequences of conventional state aggression.

A strategy that addresses nuclear weapons while permitting Iran’s proxy network to remain intact would solve only half the problem. Tehran could continue destabilizing neighboring countries, attacking shipping, threatening Israel, striking American forces and intimidating European governments without possessing a nuclear bomb.

The proper Western objective should therefore be straightforward: no nuclear weapon and no Iranian-controlled proxy network operating with impunity.

Europe’s first responsibility is to support that objective publicly and without qualification.

Its second is to provide practical assistance.

European governments should make allied bases fully available, without political caveats designed for domestic consumption. They should expand intelligence sharing, logistics support, missile-defense cooperation and maritime surveillance. European navies can provide minesweepers, escort vessels, patrol aircraft and other capabilities needed to protect international shipping.

Freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz is not an American charity project. It is an economic necessity for Europe. European manufacturers, consumers and energy markets all depend on stable global commerce. If Iran can mine waterways, seize vessels or manipulate energy prices without a united response, Europeans will bear a substantial share of the cost.

Countries that deny access to bases or obstruct allied operations should face political consequences. Spain, in particular, should not be permitted to benefit from the protection of the Atlantic alliance while frustrating efforts directed at a common threat. Alliance membership entails obligations, not merely benefits.

This is the broader meaning of burden-sharing.

President Trump has long argued that Europe must assume more responsibility for its defense. The debate has usually centered on military spending as a percentage of gross domestic product. But burden-sharing is not simply an accounting exercise. It also means standing with the U.S. when common security interests require difficult action.

For generations, Americans have accepted extraordinary commitments to protect Europe. They have stationed troops on the continent, maintained nuclear deterrence, funded military infrastructure and repeatedly made clear that an attack on Europe would be treated as an attack on the U.S.

Reciprocity demands more than gratitude. It demands solidarity when America confronts a threat that Europe itself has repeatedly identified.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

Critics will argue that stronger European support risks escalation. But the opposite is at least as likely. Weakness, hesitation and division invite miscalculation. They encourage authoritarian governments to believe that democracies lack the will to defend their stated interests.

Deterrence works when an adversary believes its opponents possess both the capacity and the unity to act.

For too long, Europe has attempted to separate diplomacy from power. It has treated negotiation as an alternative to leverage rather than as a means of employing it. The result has been years of agreements, sanctions relief, diplomatic outreach and warnings, while Iran continued enriching uranium, improving missiles and expanding its proxy apparatus.

Diplomacy remains necessary. But diplomacy unsupported by credible force becomes little more than managed delay.

No responsible leader should welcome war with Iran. The objective should not be permanent conflict or regime-change adventurism. It should be to destroy the capabilities that threaten the West and restore deterrence on terms Tehran understands.

Europe has already concluded that Iran poses an unacceptable danger. Its leaders have said so for years.

The question is no longer whether Europe understands the threat.

It is whether Europe is finally prepared to act as though it does.
